name: jesse-dydx-v3-python

metadata.name: jesse-dydx-v3-python
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License
Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: dYdX Python REST API for Limit Orders

documentation: Python client for dYdX (v3 API). The library is currently tested against Python versions 2.7, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, 3.9, and 3.11. ## Installation The `dydx-v3-python` package is available on [PyPI](https://pypi.org/project/dydx-v3-python). Install with `pip`: ```bash pip install dydx-v3-python ``` ## Getting Started The `Client` object can be created with different levels of authentication depending on which features are needed. For more complete examples, see the [examples](./examples/) directory, as well as [the integration tests](./integration_tests/). ### Public endpoints No authentication information is required to access public endpoints. ```python from dydx3 import Client from web3 import Web3 # # Access public API endpoints. # public_client = Client( host='http://localhost:8080', ) public_client.public.get_markets() ``` ### Private endpoints One of the following is required: * `api_key_credentials` * `eth_private_key` * `web3` * `web3_account` * `web3_provider` ```python # # Access private API endpoints, without providing a STARK private key. # private_client = Client( host='http://localhost:8080', api_key_credentials={ 'key': '...', ... }, ) private_client.private.get_orders() private_client.private.create_order( # No STARK key, so signatures are required for orders and withdrawals. signature='...', # ... ) # # Access private API endpoints, with a STARK private key. # private_client_with_key = Client( host='http://localhost:8080', api_key_credentials={ 'key': '...', ... }, stark_private_key='...', ) private_client.private.create_order( # Order will be signed using the provided STARK private key. # ... ) ``` ### Onboarding and API key management endpoints One of the following is required: * `eth_private_key` * `web3` * `web3_account` * `web3_provider` ```python # # Onboard a new user or manage API keys, without providing private keys. # web3_client = Client( host='http://localhost:8080', web3_provider=Web3.HTTPProvider('http://localhost:8545'), ) web3_client.onboarding.create_user( stark_public_key='...', ethereum_address='...', ) web3_client.eth_private.create_api_key( ethereum_address='...', ) # # Onboard a new user or manage API keys, with private keys. # web3_client_with_keys = Client( host='http://localhost:8080', stark_private_key='...', eth_private_key='...', ) web3_client_with_keys.onboarding.create_user() web3_client_with_keys.eth_private.create_api_key() ``` ### Using the C++ Library for STARK Signing By default, STARK curve operations such as signing and verification will use the Python native implementation. These operations occur whenever placing an order or requesting a withdrawal. To use the C++ implementation, initialize the client object with `crypto_c_exports_path`: ```python client = Client( crypto_c_exports_path='./libcrypto_c_exports.so', ... ) ``` The path should point to a C++ shared library file, built from Starkware's `crypto-cpp` library ([CMake target](https://github.com/starkware-libs/crypto-cpp/blob/601de408bee9f897315b8a5cb0c88e2450a91282/src/starkware/crypto/ffi/CMakeLists.txt#L3)) for the particular platform (e.g. Linux, etc.) that you are running your trading program on. ## Running tests If you want to run tests when developing the library locally, clone the repo and run: ``` pip install -r requirements.txt docker-compose up # In a separate terminal V3_API_HOST= tox ``` NOTE: `api-host` should be `https://api.stage.dydx.exchange` to test in staging.

classes.name: dydx3.errors.DydxError
documentation:
Base error class for all exceptions raised in this library.
Will never be raised naked; more specific subclasses of this exception will
be raised when appropriate.

methods.name: dydx3.modules.eth_private.EthPrivate.recovery
documentation:
This is for if you can't recover your starkKey or apiKey and need an
additional way to get your starkKey and balance on our exchange,
both of which are needed to call the L1 solidity function needed to
recover your funds.

```
param ethereum_address

optional

type ethereum_address

str

returns

source:9: (INFO/1) Unexpected possible title overline or transition.
Treating it as ordinary text because it's so short.

{
starkKey: str,
positionId: str,
equity: str,
freeCollateral: str,
quoteBalance: str,
positions: array of open positions
```

}

```
raises

DydxAPIError
```
Module for adding/deleting API keys and recovery.

methods.name: dydx3.modules.onboarding.Onboarding.create_user
documentation:
Onboard a user with an Ethereum address and STARK key.

By default, onboards using the STARK and/or API public keys
corresponding to private keys that the client was initialized with.

```
param stark_public_key

optional

type stark_public_key

str

param stark_public_key_y_coordinate

str

str
```

\:param country optional

```
returns

{ apiKey, user, account }
```

methods.name: dydx3.modules.onboarding.Onboarding.derive_stark_key
documentation:
Derive a STARK key pair deterministically from an Ethereum key.

This is the function used by the dYdX frontend to derive a user's
STARK key pair in a way that is recoverable. Programmatic traders may
optionally derive their STARK key pair in the same way.

```
param ethereum_address

optional

type ethereum_address

str
```

methods.name: dydx3.modules.onboarding.Onboarding.recover_default_api_key_credentials
documentation:
Derive API credentials deterministically from an Ethereum key.

This can be used to recover the default API key credentials, which are
the same set of credentials used in the dYdX frontend.

documentation: Convert a bytestring to an int.

documentation: Normalize to a 32-byte hex string without 0x prefix.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.helpers.serialize_signature
documentation: Convert a signature from an r, s pair to a 32-byte hex string.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.helpers.deserialize_signature
documentation: Convert a signature from a 32-byte hex string to an r, s pair.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.helpers.to_quantums_exact
documentation:
Convert a human-readable amount to an integer amount of quantums.

If the provided human_amount is not a multiple of the quantum size,
an exception will be raised.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.helpers.to_quantums_round_down
documentation:
Convert a human-readable amount to an integer amount of quantums.

If the provided human_amount is not a multiple of the quantum size,
the result will be rounded down to the nearest integer.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.helpers.to_quantums_round_up
documentation:
Convert a human-readable amount to an integer amount of quantums.

If the provided human_amount is not a multiple of the quantum size,
the result will be rounded up to the nearest integer.

modules.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources
documentation:
Cryptographic functions to sign and verify signatures with STARK keys.

The code in this module is copied from
    https://github.com/starkware-libs/starkex-resources
and is used in accordance with the Apache License, Version 2.0.

https://www.starkware.co/open-source-license/

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.cpp_signature.cpp_hash
documentation: CPP WRAPPERS #

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.cpp_signature.cpp_sign
documentation:
Note that this uses the secrets module to generate cryptographically strong random numbers.
Note that the same seed will give a different signature compared with the sign function in
signature.py.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.pi_as_string
documentation: Returns pi as a string of decimal digits without the decimal point ("314...").

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.is_quad_residue
documentation: Returns True if n is a quadratic residue mod p.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.sqrt_mod
documentation: Finds the minimum positive integer m such that (m*m) % p == n

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.div_mod
documentation: Finds a nonnegative integer 0 <= x < p such that (m * x) % p == n

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.ec_add
documentation:
Gets two points on an elliptic curve mod p and returns their sum.
Assumes the points are given in affine form (x, y) and have different x coordinates.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.ec_neg
documentation: Given a point (x,y) return (x, -y)

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.ec_double
documentation:
Doubles a point on an elliptic curve with the equation y^2 = x^3 + alpha*x + beta mod p.
Assumes the point is given in affine form (x, y) and has y != 0.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.math_utils.ec_mult
documentation:
Multiplies by m a point on the elliptic curve with equation y^2 = x^3 + alpha*x + beta mod p.
Assumes the point is given in affine form (x, y) and that 0 < m < order(point).

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.python_signature.get_y_coordinate
documentation:
Given the x coordinate of a stark_key, returns a possible y coordinate such that together the
point (x,y) is on the curve.
Note that the real y coordinate is either y or -y.
If x is invalid stark_key it throws an error.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.python_signature.py_sign
documentation: Starkware crypto functions implemented in Python.
Copied from:
https://github.com/starkware-libs/starkex-resources/blob/0f08e6c55ad88c93499f71f2af4a2e7ae0185cdf/crypto/starkware/crypto/signature/signature.py
Changes made by dYdX to function name only.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.python_signature.mimic_ec_mult_air
documentation:
Computes m * point + shift_point using the same steps like the AIR and throws an exception if
and only if the AIR errors.

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.python_signature.py_verify
documentation: Starkware crypto functions implemented in Python.
Copied from:
https://github.com/starkware-libs/starkex-resources/blob/0f08e6c55ad88c93499f71f2af4a2e7ae0185cdf/crypto/starkware/crypto/signature/signature.py
Changes made by dYdX to function name only.

documentation: Pedersen hash #
Starkware crypto functions implemented in Python.
Copied from:

functions.name: dydx3.starkex.starkex_resources.python_signature.pedersen_hash_as_point
documentation:
Similar to pedersen_hash but also returns the y coordinate of the resulting EC point.
This function is used for testing.
